title: Extended Theories of Electrodynamics in $f(R)$ Gravity
comment: 12 Pages; Accepted for publication in Annals of Physics
summary: Within the general framework of $f(R)$ gravity, we introduce a function of the electromagnetic curvature invariant $f(\mathbb{F})$ that couples minimally to gravitation to ensure a consistent treatment of curvature functions in these theories. We show that one of the solutions leads to field equations that are a generalization of the Klein-Gordon equation while the other leads to a typically non-linear massless solution. Focusing on flat spacetime, our formalism recovers the Plebanski family of models and Bopp-Podolsky electrodynamics as specific limits. These extensions may have phenomenological consequences in extreme environments, such as the early universe or near charged compact objects, where deviations from classical electrodynamics might be probed.

AI.tldr: The paper explores $f(R)$ gravity by introducing an electromagnetic curvature invariant $f(\mathbb{F})$ minimally coupled to gravity, deriving solutions analogous to Klein-Gordon equations and non-linear massless solutions. It recovers known models in flat spacetime and suggests potential phenomenological impacts in extreme environments.
AI.motivation: To address inconsistencies in how curvature functions are treated in $f(R)$ gravity theories by introducing a minimal coupling with an electromagnetic curvature invariant $f(\mathbb{F})$, enabling a more consistent framework that can explain phenomena where classical electrodynamics may fail.
AI.method: The authors formulate a modified $f(R)$ gravity framework incorporating $f(\mathbb{F})$ minimally coupled to gravity. They derive field equations under this setup, analyze solutions in flat spacetime, and show recovery of Plebanski models and Bopp-Podolsky electrodynamics as limiting cases.
AI.result: Two key solutions were obtained: one generalizing the Klein-Gordon equation and another yielding a non-linear massless solution. These results unify previous models (Plebanski, Bopp-Podolsky) within the $f(R)$ framework and highlight potential observational effects in extreme spacetime conditions.
AI.conclusion: The developed formalism provides a consistent extension of $f(R)$ gravity with electromagnetic interactions, offering testable predictions in strong-field regimes like the early universe or around charged compact objects where electrodynamics deviations could be observed.

title: The imitation game (r)evolutions: $Q$-star effective shadow from GRMHD analysis
summary: $Q$-stars are a class of boson stars arising in scalar-field theories with interacting potentials, minimally coupled to gravity. We show that, in certain regions of parameter space, the angular velocity of stable timelike circular geodesics around $Q$-stars can attain a maximum at a nonzero radius. Notably, this behaviour may occur for stable configurations. This feature has been argued to produce effective shadows, but so far it has only been investigated for unstable solutions. We test this possibility by performing general relativistic magnetohydrodynamic evolutions for a representative stable $Q$-star model. A low-density, low-luminosity central region is indeed observed to form and persist -- at least until the evolution becomes affected by numerical viscosity. As a proof of principle, this suggests that families of stable bosonic stars can act as black hole mimickers. Moreover, for the model at hand, a heuristic analysis shows that the effective shadow has a comparable size to that of a Schwarzschild black hole with the same mass. Importantly, this mechanism for generating an effective shadow does not rely on the object being ultracompact, or an ad hoc chosen accretion disk.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether stable Q-stars, a type of boson star, can mimic black holes by creating effective shadows through their gravitational signatures.
AI.motivation: To determine if stable Q-stars, which are boson stars with potential-induced properties, can generate observable features like black hole shadows without requiring ultracompactness or artificial accretion disks.
AI.method: The authors performed general relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of a stable Q-star model to observe the formation and persistence of a low-density region resembling a shadow. They analyzed the angular velocity profiles of geodesics around the Q-star and compared the shadow size to a Schwarzschild black hole of equal mass.
AI.result: Simulations showed that a low-density, low-luminosity central region (an effective shadow) forms and persists in stable Q-stars, at least until numerical viscosity intervenes. The shadow's size is comparable to that of a Schwarzschild black hole with the same mass.
AI.conclusion: Stable Q-stars can act as black hole mimickers via this mechanism, which doesn't require the object to be ultracompact or have special accretion disks. This expands the potential observational signatures of compact objects beyond traditional black hole candidates.

title: The Structure of the Continuum Limit of Spin Foams
summary: The Spin Foam approach to quantum gravity aims at providing a covariant path-integral formulation of canonical Loop Quantum Gravity. Since spin foam amplitudes are defined through discretisations of spacetime, understanding the continuum limit of the theory remains a central open problem. In this work, we investigate the structural aspects of this limit in a model-independent manner. We begin by introducing an axiomatic framework for spin foam amplitudes inspired by Atiyah's formulation of Topological Quantum Field Theories (TQFTs). In this setting, Hilbert spaces and amplitudes are assigned to combinatorial and topological data associated with triangulated manifolds. By equipping the set of triangulations with suitable orders, this framework provides a precise notion of continuum limit and allows us to analyse its properties independently of any specific model. We proceed then to systematically investigate how the specifics of the limit procedure allow to go beyond TQFT in the continuum. Under natural assumptions on the convergence of spin foam amplitudes, we establish a no-go result: sufficiently strong notions of convergence necessarily lead to a topological theory. Motivated by this obstruction, we weaken the notion of convergence and consider the continuum limit of spin foam amplitudes in a distributional sense, in the spirit of Refined Algebraic Quantisation. Under this assumption, the amplitude associated with the cylinder defines a rigging map, yielding a canonical construction of the physical Hilbert space. The resulting continuum amplitudes act as well-defined distributions on this space of physical states, characterising this formulation of the gravitational path integral as physical in a precise sense.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the continuum limit of spin foam models in quantum gravity, proposing an axiomatic framework inspired by TQFTs and showing that strict convergence implies topological theories, necessitating a distributional approach for physicality.
AI.motivation: The central problem of understanding the continuum limit of spin foam models, which is essential for their validity as a quantum gravity theory. Existing approaches struggle to move beyond topological quantum field theories (TQFTs) in the continuum.
AI.method: 1. Axiomatic framework based on Atiyah's TQFT formalism for spin foam amplitudes and triangulations. 2. Analysis of convergence conditions for continuum limits. 3. Distributional rigging map construction via Refined Algebraic Quantisation to bypass TQFT obstruction.
AI.result: 1. Strict continuum convergence necessarily leads to TQFTs (no-go theorem). 2. A distributional approach using cylinder amplitudes defines a physical Hilbert space and valid continuum amplitudes.
AI.conclusion: Physical quantum gravity requires distributional continuum limits; the proposed framework provides a mathematically consistent path without topological constraints by using rigging maps and weakened convergence criteria.

title: The two shadows of a single black hole: Vacuum birefringence phenomena within Einstein-Nonlinear-Electrodynamics
summary: One of the main features of nonlinear electrodynamics (NED) is the existence of an effective geometry that describes the geodesic motion of photons. A detailed analysis of the properties of effective geometry is of utmost importance for a better understanding of NED theories and their possible imprints on physics, especially in the context of black holes (BHs). We consider a NED model that depends on the two electromagnetic scalar invariants and obtain that the motion of photons in NED exhibits \textit{vacuum birefringence}, i.e., photons can propagate along two distinct paths, depending on their polarization. As a consequence of this phenomenon, we show that static black hole solutions sourced by NED can admit two distinct unstable light rings, leading to the formation of two distinct shadows. Moreover, to explore the potential astrophysical relevance of our results, we also compare them with the astrophysical observations for the shadow radius of Sagittarius A*. We place upper limits on the charge-to-mass ratio of the NED-sourced black hole. We also show that the motion of photons in this context can be interpreted as nongeodesic curves subjected to a four-force term from the perspective of an observer in the spacetime metric, generalizing previous results in the literature for NED models that depend on a single electromagnetic scalar invariant.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates nonlinear electrodynamics (NED)-induced vacuum birefringence affecting photon paths and black hole shadows, showing two unstable light rings and dual shadows, and constrains parameters using Sagittarius A* data.
AI.motivation: To understand NED's impact on black hole physics, particularly through vacuum birefringence and its observational signatures like shadow variability.
AI.method: Analyzing a two-invariant NED model, deriving photon trajectories, calculating light rings, and comparing with Sagittarius A* observations.
AI.result: Photons exhibit vacuum birefringence with two distinct paths; NED black holes have two unstable light rings and dual shadows; upper limits set on charge-to-mass ratio.
AI.conclusion: NED modifies black hole shadow structures and photon dynamics, offering testable predictions for future astrophysical observations with instruments like the EHT.

title: Higher-point Energy Correlators: Factorization in the Back-to-Back Limit & Non-perturbative Effects
summary: N-point energy correlators are powerful observables for studying strong interactions, with applications ranging from extractions of the strong coupling $α_s$ to probes of jet modification in heavy-ion collisions and determination of the top-quark mass. Their practical use has, however, been limited by the complicated phase space for large N. Using a recently introduced parametrization that simplifies this structure, we study projected N-point correlators in two regimes: factorization in the back-to-back limit and leading non-perturbative effects in the collinear limit. While results in the back-to-back regime were previously limited to the energy-energy correlator, our approach allows us to derive the factorization theorem for arbitrary N. We compute the new ingredient, a one-loop jet function, needed for the next-to-next-to-leading-logarithmic resummation, which enables future $α_s$ extractions with complementary systematics. We further determine the analytic structure of leading non-perturbative power corrections for arbitrary N, including their dependence on the center-of-mass energy Q, the value of N, and the angular scale $x$. We present the first results for non-integer N<1, finding that the classical scaling in $x$ acquires an N-dependent modification, and that a new non-perturbative matrix element $\tildeΩ^{[N]}$ appears. In a certain approximation, $\tildeΩ^{[N]}$ can be related to the standard parameter $Ω_1$ relevant for N>1. Our analytic predictions are tested against the hadronization model in Pythia, finding good agreement. The results presented in this paper demonstrate the significant advancements enabled through our new parametrization of energy correlators.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new parametrization method for N-point energy correlators to simplify phase space complexity, enabling factorization in back-to-back and collinear limits, and derives novel non-perturbative effects. This advances the analysis of strong interactions, α_s measurements, and top-quark mass determinations.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations in using N-point correlators due to complex phase space for large N and extend applicability beyond the energy-energy correlator (EEC) for precision studies in QCD.
AI.method: Developed a simplified parametrization for N-point correlators, derived factorization theorems for arbitrary N in back-to-back limits, computed one-loop jet functions, and analyzed non-perturbative power corrections in collinear limits. Also explored non-integer N values and their dependence on parameters like Q, N, and x.
AI.result: First derivation of factorization for arbitrary N in back-to-back limits, introduced N-dependent non-perturbative matrix element $\tildeΩ^{[N]}$, demonstrated N<1 scaling behavior modifications, achieved agreement with Pythia simulations.
AI.conclusion: The new parametrization significantly enhances the analytical and computational feasibility of N-point correlators, providing tools for precision QCD measurements and future α_s extractions with improved systematics.

title: Search For a Counterpart to the Subsolar Mass Gravitational Wave Candidate S251112cm
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures in body; submitted to ApJ; comments welcome!
summary: The recent gravitational-wave (GW) alert from a compact object merger involving at least one subsolar mass (SSM) object has prompted questions about their origins. S251112cm is reported by LIGO/Virgo with a false alarm rate of 1 per 6.2 years, nearby luminosity distance $93 \pm 27$ Mpc, probability of containing a SSM object of 100%, and probability of containing a $1-3~M_\odot$ object of just 8%. Such a system likely did not involve the supersolar neutron stars or black holes invoked to explain kilonovae. One must then also invoke hitherto unobserved and speculative models to produce SSM mergers and the resultant electromagnetic (EM) counterparts. We introduce a framework which vets and scores candidate counterparts to SSM GW events to inform follow-up in search of any among the zoo of potential EM transients: kilonovae, kilonovae-within-supernovae, super-kilonovae, or AGN flares from binary black hole mergers. We use a suite of telescopes to perform tiling, galaxy-targeted observations, and photometric/spectroscopic follow-up of promising candidates. In near-real time, we ingest candidates reported by the community, including some of the first observations reported by the Vera C. Rubin Observatory. We vet and score a total of 248 candidates, including 67 from Rubin, but find no likely counterpart. We nonetheless highlight candidates which demonstrate the ability of our framework to distinguish between different transient types and describe strategies to maximize the chances of detecting a counterpart to the next SSM event. Our framework will be implemented in the forthcoming Multimessenger Tool for Rapid Object Vetting and Examination (TROVE).

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the analysis of gravitational-wave event S251112cm involving subsolar mass objects, introducing a framework to evaluate potential electromagnetic counterparts and conclude no likely counterpart was found among 248 candidates.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainties about the origin of subsolar mass mergers and their electromagnetic counterparts, prompted by the unusual S251112cm event lacking traditional explanations.
AI.method: Develop a vetting framework scoring candidates using telescope observations (including Rubin Observatory data), analyzing 248 candidates through tiling, galaxy-targeted observations, and photometric/spectroscopic follow-up.
AI.result: No credible electromagnetic counterpart identified, but demonstrated the framework's ability to distinguish transient types and inform future detection strategies.
AI.conclusion: Current data can't confirm an electromagnetic counterpart, but the framework (to be part of TROVE tool) provides robust methods for upcoming subsolar mass merger analyses.

title: A Minimal Four-Thruster System for Comet-Based Interstellar Navigation
comment: 4 figures, 4 tables, 2 appendices with supporting proofs
summary: Interstellar comets arrive with key ingredients for deep-space platforms already in place: volatile inventories convertible to propellant, natural rotation providing continuous attitude variation, and hyperbolic trajectories that carry them through the inner Solar System and back out to interstellar space. Rather than constructing spacecraft from scratch, we ask what \emph{minimal modification} is required to steer such a body along a controlled trajectory. The answer is surprisingly modest. By relaxing full six-degree-of-freedom control to forward-cone steering -- sufficient for practical navigation -- we show that \emph{four thrusters suffice}: one primary jet and three secondary jets at $120^\circ$ intervals. The secondary jets synthesize continuous in-plane steering, while the primary jet provides low-bandwidth attitude shaping: as the body rotates, the primary-jet torque direction sweeps predictably over a cycle, enabling out-of-plane steering via phase-scheduled firing. We formalize reachability under bounded-curvature constraints, characterize the rotation-mediated steering envelope, discuss enabling requirements including non-solar power at large heliocentric distances, and identify operational regimes and observable signatures implied by active trajectory control. The setting of a nutating axis is briefly considered and conjectured to preserve core results. The findings contribute to the broader effort of understanding the dynamics and control of small-body missions and offer a reference architecture relevant to long-horizon deep-space exploration and to potential planetary-defense concepts.

AI.tldr: The paper explores steering an interstellar comet with minimal modification using four thrusters for controlled trajectory navigation, enabling deep-space exploration and planetary defense.
AI.motivation: Leveraging cometary resources (volatiles, rotation, trajectory) to reduce spacecraft construction costs and enhance mission capabilities for deep-space missions and planetary defense.
AI.method: Proposes a system with one primary and three secondary thrusters arranged at 120° intervals. Secondary thrusters handle in-plane steering, while the primary jet uses the comet's rotation for out-of-plane control via phase-scheduled firing. Reachability under curvature constraints and operational requirements are analyzed.
AI.result: Demonstrates that four thrusters suffice for forward-cone steering, enabling controllable trajectories. Identifies power needs at large distances and observable signatures of controlled comets. Rotation-based steering maintains core control functionalities.
AI.conclusion: The minimal intervention approach opens new possibilities for comet-based missions, supporting long-term deep-space exploration and planetary defense strategies. The architecture provides a reference for future small-body mission designs.

title: The EPRL amplitude is supported on flat connections
summary:
For the version of the EPRL model based on the original vertex amplitude and the face amplitude selected by its gluing properties, we prove that the EPRL amplitude of any region with the topology of a 4-ball is supported on flat connections. We state immediate consequences of this result, comment on some applications, and discuss physical implications.
  The results hold in general; they do not rely on a semiclassical analysis.

AI.tldr: The analysis of the EPRL model's amplitudes reveals that the theory's 4-ball topology regions support flat connections without requiring semiclassical approaches.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the quantum geometry aspects of the EPRL model by examining its amplitudes and their implications for background-independent quantum gravity.
AI.method: The analysis focuses on the EPRL model with original vertex and face amplitudes, proving that the 4-ball region amplitudes are supported on flat connections through direct algebraic/topological methods.
AI.result: The EPRL amplitude for 4-ball topology regions is strictly supported on flat connections, indicating a connection between the model's structure and classical flatness without semiclassical limits.
AI.conclusion: This result underscores the model's consistency with classical general relativity in specific topologies and suggests potential for deeper insights into quantum gravity's dynamics without relying on perturbative or semiclassical techniques.

title: Is the Turner Window Open? Seeking Closure with Resonant Absorption of Galactic Axions in NaI Dark Matter Detectors
summary: Motivated by the DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation signal, the dark matter community has invested heavily in ultra-clean underground NaI detectors to search for light WIMPs. We point out a new target of opportunity for these detectors -- axions produced by the carbon-burning stars within our galaxy. These stars synthesize large quantities of $^{23}$Na, keeping it at temperatures $\sim 10^9$K for periods up to tens of thousands of years. Under these conditions, $^{23}$Na radiates 440 keV axions through repeated photo-excitation and axio-deexcitation of its first excited state. Upon reaching a NaI detector, the process is reversed: the axion is resonantly absorbed, producing a 440 keV deexcitation photon. NaI thus serves as both $γ$ source and $γ$ detector. We find that existing NaI detectors can probe axion-nucleon couplings $|g_{aNN}^\mathrm{eff~^{23}Na}| \approx g_{app} \sim 10^{-6}$--$10^{-2}$, including QCD axions with $m_a \gtrsim 10$ eV. While there are several astrophysical constraints on axions with these couplings, our re-examination of these bounds shows that substantial gaps remain, providing strong motivation for the proposed searches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using existing NaI detectors to search for axions produced in carbon-burning stars, utilizing 440 keV axion-photon conversions. This technique could probe axion-nucleon couplings within detectable ranges and complement astrophysical constraints.
AI.motivation: The DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation signal motivated searches for light WIMPs, but the authors identify a new opportunity to detect axions using the same NaI detectors. Axions from carbon-burning stars interacting with $^{23}$Na provide an accessible target for existing infrastructure.
AI.method: Exploits $^{23}Na's ability to emit 440 keV axions via photon-axion oscillations in stellar cores. Axions travel to Earth and are reabsorbed in NaI detectors, creating detectable photon signals. Calculations estimate detectable axion-nucleon coupling ranges.
AI.result: Existing NaI detectors can probe $|g_{aNN}^	ext{eff}|	extsuperscript{23}Na ~g_{app} ~10^{-6}$--$10^{-2}$, covering QCD axions above ~10 eV. While some astrophysical constraints exist, significant parameter space remains unexplored.
AI.conclusion: Urges deployment of ultra-clean NaI detectors for axion searches as a high-impact, low-cost avenue given existing infrastructure investments. Highlights the synergy between dark matter WIMP and axion research using shared resources.

title: Ultrahigh-Energy Gamma-Ray Sources Need Not Be Hadronic PeVatrons
summary: Ultrahigh-energy gamma rays ($E_γ>100 \, {\rm TeV}$) have been detected from a handful of astrophysical sources. Due to the Klein-Nishina suppression of inverse Compton scattering at such high energies, it has sometimes been argued that these sources must be accelerators of PeV-scale protons, making them the long-sought-after Galactic ''PeVatrons.'' Here, we challenge this conclusion, demonstrating that these sources can be straightforwardly explained by simple leptonic models. In this context, we consider the microquasar SS 433, the Galactic Center, and TeV halos, showing in each case that the observation of PeV-scale gamma rays from these sources does not indicate that they are accelerators of hadronic cosmic rays. We also note that the measured angular extension of SS 433 is in good agreement with the predictions of our model, favoring a leptonic origin for the gamma-ray emission from this source. A definitive identification of a PeVatron would require additional information, such as the combined observation of the pion bump and synchrotron peak, the spatial correlation of gamma-ray emission with gas, or the detection of neutrinos with $E_ν \gtrsim 100 \, {\rm TeV}$.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the assumption that ultrahigh-energy gamma-ray sources (E>100 TeV) must originate from hadronic PeV protons (PeVatrons), showing they can instead arise from leptonic processes in sources like SS 433, the Galactic Center, and TeV halos. It emphasizes needing additional evidence like pion bumps, gas correlations, or neutrinos to confirm PeVatrons.
AI.motivation: Many astrophysical sources emitting gamma rays above 100 TeV are thought to be PeVatrons accelerating protons to PeV energies due to Klein-Nishina suppression in inverse Compton scattering. The authors aim to demonstrate that these observations can be explained by simpler leptonic models without requiring hadronic acceleration.
AI.method: The study examines three sources (SS 433, Galactic Center, TeV halos) using leptonic models. They model gamma-ray production via leptonic processes (electron/positron acceleration and magnetic field interactions) and compare predicted spectra/morphologies with observations. Angular extension of SS 433 matches model predictions for leptonic origin.
AI.result: Leptonic models successfully explain the PeV gamma-ray emission from all three sources without requiring hadronic acceleration. SS 433's observed angular size aligns with the model. The paper shows that current data do not conclusively prove the existence of PeVatrons.
AI.conclusion: PeV-scale gamma rays alone aren't sufficient evidence for hadronic PeVatrons. Additional signatures like pion decay features, spatial gas correlations, or TeV neutrinos are needed to definitively identify PeVatron sources.

title: Trajectory Stability and Signature Diagnostics for Comet-Based Interstellar Navigation
summary: Interstellar objects (ISOs) motivate a coupled mission-design and inference question relevant to spacecraft dynamics and control in extreme environments: if volatile-rich, rotating comet-like bodies were used for sustained deep-space navigation by exploiting pre-existing hyperbolic motion and in-situ propellant, what stability requirements arise under non-gravitational forcing, and what astrometric signatures might distinguish active stabilization from uncontrolled natural dynamics? We develop a stability-theoretic framework for trajectory tracking with jet-actuated correction, and show that high-speed transit geometry -- including debris-belt avoidance and encounter phasing -- tightly constrains feasible trajectories, making long-horizon tracking stability mission-critical. We model tracking residuals as the balance of disturbances and corrective action, and derive stability conditions across four levels: disturbance-energy stability, outer-loop contraction, actuator-memory stability, and rotation-mediated (Floquet) stability. The analysis implies residual diagnostics that can motivate empirical tests: under comparable forcing, effective stabilization is expected to strengthen short-horizon error correction, reduce event-conditioned persistence and variance clustering, regularize standardized innovations, and yield bounded post-shock recovery. More broadly, the framework provides a reference for deep-space guidance and control under nonlinear, multi-field disturbances and for planetary-defense concepts involving attitude shaping or impulsive kinetic impact.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using volatile-rich, rotating comet-like interstellar objects (ISOs) for deep-space navigation by leveraging their natural hyperbolic motion and in-situ propellant. It develops a stability framework for jet-actuated trajectory tracking, identifies mission-critical stability requirements, and proposes astrometric signatures to distinguish controlled stabilization from natural dynamics.
AI.motivation: ISOs represent a novel opportunity for sustainable deep-space missions. Their inherent hyperbolic trajectories and volatile-rich composition could provide propellant, but their stability under non-gravitational forces and trajectory tracking challenges in extreme environments (e.g., high-speed transits, debris avoidance) require rigorous analysis to enable controlled navigation.
AI.method: A stability-theoretic framework is developed to analyze trajectory tracking with jet corrections, considering disturbances from non-gravitational forces. Stability conditions are derived across four levels: disturbance-energy, outer-loop contraction, actuator-memory, and rotation-mediated Floquet stability. Astrometric signatures for empirical validation are proposed.
AI.result: The framework shows that high-speed transit constraints make long-horizon stability mission-critical. Stabilization improves short-term error correction, reduces variance clustering, and ensures bounded recovery post-impacts. Residual diagnostics provide testable predictions distinguishing controlled vs. uncontrolled dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The analysis supports feasible deep-space guidance using ISOs but emphasizes strict stability requirements. The methods apply broadly to planetary defense (e.g., attitude shaping) and missions involving nonlinear multi-field disturbances.

title: Scattering of a scalar field in the four-dimensional quasi-topological gravity
summary: We study grey-body factors for a massless scalar field in the spacetime of regular black holes arising in four-dimensional non-polynomial quasi-topological gravity. We consider two representative metrics that capture the typical features of regular geometries. Using the WKB method, we compute the transmission probabilities and analyze their dependence on the regularization parameter. The grey-body factors are found to deviate only slightly from the Schwarzschild case, indicating that the scattering properties are largely insensitive to near-horizon regularization of the geometry. The correspondence between quasinormal modes and grey-body factors is shown to be sufficiently accurate for higher multipole numbers.

AI.tldr: The paper examines grey-body factors for a massless scalar field in regular black hole spacetimes within non-polynomial quasi-topological gravity, finding minimal deviations from Schwarzschild results and strong QNM-greedy-body factor correlation for higher multipoles.
AI.motivation: To understand how regularizing black hole geometries affects scattering properties and grey-body factors, testing the sensitivity of transmission probabilities to spacetime regularization parameters.
AI.method: Applied the WKB method to two representative regular black hole metrics in non-polynomial quasi-topological gravity, computationally deriving transmission probabilities and analyzing their dependence on regularization parameters.
AI.result: Grey-body factors showed only minor differences from Schwarzschild results, indicating scattering properties are largely insensitive to near-horizon regularization. Quasinormal modes and grey-body factors were found to correlate accurately for higher multipole numbers.
AI.conclusion: Regularization of black hole spacetimes in this gravity model doesn't significantly affect scattering outcomes, and QNM-greedy-body factor correspondence remains valid for high multipole scenarios, supporting robust theoretical frameworks.

title: Opening up baryon-number-violating operators
comment: 25+118 pages; code and data files available at https://uva.theopenscholar.com/heeck/BNV
summary: Baryon number violation is our most sensitive probe of physics beyond the Standard Model. Its realization through heavy new particles can be conveniently encoded in higher-dimensional operators that allow for model-agnostic analyses. The unparalleled sensitivity of nuclear decays to baryon number violation makes it possible to probe effective operators of very high mass dimension, far beyond the commonly discussed dimension-six operators. To facilitate studies of this ginormous and scarcely explored testable operator landscape we provide the exhaustive set of tree-level UV completions consisting of scalars, fermions, and vectors for non-derivative baryon-number-violating operators in this Standard Model effective field theory up to mass dimension 15, which corresponds roughly to the border of sensitivity. In addition to the known Standard Model fields we also include right-handed neutrinos in our operators. Our public code can be used to UV-complete any non-derivative operator and match it onto an operator basis.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a comprehensive analysis of non-derivative baryon-number-violating operators in the Standard Model effective field theory up to mass dimension 15. It offers a systematic exploration of their ultraviolet (UV) completions using scalar, fermion, and vector particles, extending beyond the commonly studied dimension-six operators. The work includes right-handed neutrinos and provides a public code tool for operator basis matching and UV completion.
AI.motivation: To enable model-agnostic studies of baryon number violation beyond the Standard Model, focusing on higher-dimensional operators (up to dimension 15) which are accessible through sensitive probes like nuclear decays. Existing analyses often stop at dimension six, leaving a vast operator space unexplored.
AI.method: They exhaustively constructed all possible tree-level UV completions for non-derivative baryon-number-violating operators using scalars, fermions, and vectors. The analysis includes Standard Model fields plus right-handed neutrinos. A public computational tool was developed to automatically UV-complete operators and match them to an operator basis.
AI.result: The first comprehensive catalog of UV completions for baryon-violating operators up to mass dimension 15. Demonstrates feasibility of probing high-dimension operators experimentally, provides a code resource for future studies, and expands operator explorations into rarely considered dimensions.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a foundational framework for studying baryon number violation at high mass dimensions, opening new avenues for testing beyond-Standard-Model physics via nuclear decay experiments. The provided code enables researchers to efficiently analyze and UV-complete operators in this previously underexplored regime.

title: Optical outburst evolution of the transient black hole X-ray binary Swift J1727.8-1613: Disc response to jet ejections and late-outburst emergence of powerful disc winds
summary: Swift J1727.8$-$1613 is a newly discovered transient low-mass X-ray binary harbouring a stellar-mass ($\sim 10M_\odot$) black hole. We present state-resolved VLT/X-Shooter optical spectroscopy of its 2023 outburst, sampling the luminous hard-to-soft and late soft-to-hard transitions. During the onset of the brightest radio flare, He\,\textsc{ii} flux rises relative to adjacent epochs, with reduced peak-to-peak separation and full-width-half-maximum, consistent with enhanced irradiation shifting line emissivity to larger radii. We detect no contemporaneous change in the line base tracing the inner disc. The most dramatic change occurs at the onset of the dim-hard state, when strong, broad (higher-order) Balmer lines appear in absorption, and He\,\textsc{ii} remains in emission, but becomes highly asymmetric. While the hardening of the X-ray spectrum likely promotes the reappearance of an underlying disc photosphere, the kinematic alignment between the Balmer absorption ($v_w\sim-750\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$) and the suppressed blue peak of He\,\textsc{ii} suggests a unified origin in a massive, cool ($T\lesssim10^{4}\,\mathrm{K}$) accretion disc wind. Radiative transfer simulations demonstrate that such asymmetric He\,\textsc{ii} profiles are naturally produced in a rotating and accelerating outflow. Using the Sobolev approximation, we estimate the wind mass-loss rate to be $\dot{M}_w\gtrsim10^{-9}\,M_\odot\,\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$, comparable to the instantaneous accretion rate and a significant fraction of the secular mass-transfer rate from the donor. If persistent at quiescent-level X-ray luminosities, this outflow could strongly impact the system's secular evolution.

AI.tldr: The analysis of optical spectra during Swift J1727.8-1613's 2023 outburst reveals a massive, cool accretion disc wind at the hard-to-soft and soft-to-hard X-ray state transitions. The wind's kinematics link broad Balmer absorption and asymmetric He II emission, with a mass-loss rate comparable to accretion, potentially influencing long-term system evolution.
AI.motivation: To study the interplay between X-ray spectral states, accretion disc irradiation, and wind dynamics in a transient black hole X-ray binary, using high-cadence optical spectroscopy during outburst transitions.
AI.method: State-resolved VLT/X-Shooter optical spectroscopy was performed during the 2023 outburst of Swift J1727.8-1613. Observations were synchronized with X-ray spectral state transitions (hard-to-soft and soft-to-hard). Spectral line properties (He II, Balmer series) were analyzed for temporal changes in flux ratios, peak separations, and asymmetries. Radiative transfer simulations and Sobolev approximation were used to model wind origins and quantify mass-loss rates.
AI.result: Key findings include He II emission peaking during radio flares with reduced line widths (indicating outer disc irradiation shifts), sudden appearance of broad Balmer absorption blueshifted to ~-750 km/s coinciding with hard state onset, and persistent but asymmetric He II emission. Wind simulations confirm these features arise from a rotating, accelerating cool disc wind with ~1e-9 Msun/yr mass-loss rate, comparable to accretion rates.
AI.conclusion: The observed wind dynamics suggest X-ray state transitions drive mass loss from the disc via irradiation-modulated outflows. Such winds may play a critical role in angular momentum transport and secular evolution of black hole binaries by recycling material back into the disc or donor star, challenging current accretion-theory paradigms.

title: Quantifying the Scientific Potential of Intermediate and Extreme Mass Ratio Inspirals with the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna
comment: Code available at: https://github.com/lorenzsp/EMRI-FoM Interactive website: https://huggingface.co/spaces/lorenzsp/emri-imri-fom
summary: The Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) will enable precision studies of Extreme and Intermediate Mass Ratio Inspirals (EMRIs/IMRIs), providing unique probes of astrophysical environments of galactic nuclei and strong-field gravity. Using a fully relativistic pipeline across primary masses $m_1 \in [5\times10^4, 10^7]\,M_\odot$ and secondary masses $m_2 \in [1, 10^4]\,M_\odot$, we map instrumental performance directly to detection horizons and parameter measurement precision. EMRIs with $m_1 = 10^7\,M_\odot$ and $m_2 \sim 1\,M_\odot$ are the most sensitive to instrument degradation, with redshift horizons at $z \sim 0.01$, while IMRIs are the least sensitive to degradation and reach redshifts $z \sim 1-3$. All prograde systems considered achieve sub-percent spin precision within three months of observation. The full 4.5-year mission increases the horizon of systems with $m_1 = 10^7\,M_\odot$ and $m_2 \sim 1\,M_\odot$ by a factor of $\sim 4$ and improves sky localization by one to two orders of magnitude reaching $ < 10\,\mathrm{deg}^2$. IMRI detection is robust against degradation, but their parameter estimation is more vulnerable due to fewer cycles in band. With the full baseline, EMRI observations constrain scalar dipole emission and Kerr quadrupole deviations below ground-based bounds by one to two orders of magnitude. We release the accompanying software and an interactive website to enable the community to rapidly quantify the scientific potential of EMRIs and IMRIs.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how LISA can precisely study EMRIs and IMRIs to probe galactic nuclei and gravity, showing that EMRIs are more sensitive to instrument issues but achieve better parameter precision with extended observation times. IMRIs have wider redshift reach but need more cycles for accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand LISA's capability in probing astrophysical environments and testing gravity through EMRI/IMRI studies, considering how instrument performance affects results.
AI.method: A fully relativistic pipeline was used across mass ranges for primary (5e4-1e7 solar masses) and secondary (1-1e4 solar masses) bodies. Instrumental performance's impact on detection horizons and parameter precision was analyzed using simulations.
AI.result: EMRIs with high primary masses but low secondaries are most sensitive to degradation, with redshift horizons at z~0.01, while IMRIs can reach z~1-3. Full 4.5-year LISA mission improves EMRI horizons and spin precision. Scalar dipole and Kerr deviations are constrained better than ground-based observations.
AI.conclusion: LISA's potential for high-precision EMRI/IMRI studies is significant, with software/tools provided to assess scientific impact. Observations need longer durations for better results, and IMRI parameter estimation is more vulnerable despite better degradation resilience.

title: Modified Friedmann equations and non-singular cosmologies in $d=4$ non-polynomial quasi-topological gravities
summary: Quasi-topological theories of gravity are known to resolve black-hole singularities. We investigate whether the same mechanism can remove cosmological singularities. Focusing on non-polynomial curvature quasi-topological gravities in $d=4$ dimensions, we find three generic scenarios with the correct infrared limit but without a Big-Bang singularity, for universes filled with pure radiation or other standard matter. The first scenario yields a universe emerging from a de Sitter phase, a case for which the curvature invariants remain finite but the matter density diverges, albeit only at infinite affine distance. The second one corresponds to a bouncing universe, which requires a multi-valued Lagrangian. The third possibility is an asymptotically Minkowski origin, reminiscent of an eternally loitering universe. The matter energy density for this solution is non-singular even at infinite affine distance and does not enter a super-Planckian regime, but is instead approximately constant for the past eternity.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether quasi-topological gravity can resolve cosmological singularities, finding three scenarios in 4D without a Big-Bang singularity: de Sitter emergence, bouncing universe with multi-valued Lagrangian, and asymptotically Minkowski origin with constant matter density.
AI.motivation: To explore if quasi-topological gravity, known for resolving black hole singularities, can also eliminate cosmological singularities like the Big Bang.
AI.method: Analyzing non-polynomial curvature quasi-topological gravities in 4D, considering universes with radiation or standard matter and examining possible cosmological scenarios without singularities.
AI.result: Three singularity-free scenarios identified: de Sitter phase emergence (finite curvature invariants but divergent matter density at infinite affine distance), bouncing universe needing a multi-valued Lagrangian, and an asymptotically Minkowski origin with constant non-singular matter density.
AI.conclusion: Quasi-topological gravity offers viable mechanisms to avoid Big-Bang singularities through these cosmological models, suggesting alternative early-universe dynamics without initial curvature singularities.

title: SMEFT operators in rare multi-top processes
summary: Nowadays, the Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) provides a standard framework to parameterize potential deviations from the Standard Model and to combine information from multiple processes in global analyses. This review summarizes dedicated studies that constrain dimension-six Wilson coefficients using three top-quark and four top-quark production processes. We highlight the complementarity of these channels, as well as summarize the main problems and prospects in the area. A concise introduction to the SMEFT formalism and a discussion of the problem of potential perturbative unitarity violation are also provided.

AI.tldr: This review examines how top-quark production processes constrain SMEFT dimension-six Wilson coefficients, highlighting their complementarity and discussing challenges like perturbative unitarity violation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to systematically constrain SMEFT dimension-six operators using multi-top processes and unify constraints from various observables in global analyses.
AI.method: Analyzes three-and-four top-quark production channels' sensitivity to SMEFT operators, assesses their complementarity, and addresses theoretical issues like unitarity limits.
AI.result: Summarizes current constraints on Wilson coefficients, identifies which processes provide the strongest constraints, and points out unresolved theoretical challenges in SMEFT applications.
AI.conclusion: Multi-top processes are valuable probes of new physics via SMEFT, but require addressing unitarity issues and combining with other observables for comprehensive constraints.

title: Diffuse X-ray Emission in the Sagittarius C Complex
comment: 18 pages, 9 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: The Sagittarius C (Sgr C) complex, located on the western edge of the Central Molecular Zone (CMZ), hosts a mixture of star-forming and non-thermal activity whose X-ray properties remain poorly understood. Using deep archival Chandra and XMM-Newton observations, we resolve the diffuse X-ray emission in Sgr C into two components: an H II region coincident with the radio peak and a brighter diffuse feature located to its southwest. Spatially resolved spectroscopy reveals the presence of a soft (kT <= 1 keV) plasma with metal abundances consistent with the elevated metallicity expected in the CMZ in both regions, along with a harder (~ 8 keV) thermal component within the H II region. The observed diffuse X-ray emission and its association with an expanding [C II] shell suggest that the hot gas may originate from a young supernova remnant (SNR) embedded in the H II region. Under this interpretation, the inferred shock velocity (~ 800 km/s) and SNR age (>= 1.7 kyr) are consistent with a core-collapse SNR in the Galactic Center. These results reveal Sgr C as a potential host of a SNR and highlight the complex interplay between massive-star feedback, magnetic fields, and molecular gas in the CMZ.

AI.tldr: The study reveals Sgr C complex in the CMZ hosts both an H II region and a potential young supernova remnant (SNR), with the SNR's properties aligning with a core-collapse origin in the Galactic Center. Diffuse X-ray emissions, resolved into soft and hard components, suggest a complex interaction between massive star feedback and the environment.
AI.motivation: To understand the poorly studied X-ray properties of the Sgr C complex, particularly the interplay between star formation and non-thermal activity.
AI.method: Analysis of deep archival Chandra and XMM-Newton X-ray observations, spatially resolved spectroscopy to identify plasma components, and comparison with [C II] shell dynamics.
AI.result: Diffuse X-ray emission resolved into a soft plasma component (≤1 keV) with high metallicity and a harder ~8 keV component in the H II region. Shock velocity (~800 km/s) and SNR age estimates support a core-collapse SNR origin.
AI.conclusion: Sgr C likely hosts a young SNR, demonstrating the role of massive-star feedback in shaping the CMZ environment through interactions with magnetic fields and molecular gas.

title: An Analysis of Large Astronomical Detector Controller Systems and Implications for Future ESO Detector Systems
summary: Large astronomical instruments using tens to hundreds of optical or infrared science detectors pose specific challenges for detector control, where, in addition to performance, other engineering aspects like scalability, power consumption, size, weight and programmatic aspects such as cost and sustainability need to be considered. In this paper we analyze the approach existing instruments have taken for detector control. We focus this analysis on recent ground based astronomical instruments using 10 or more detectors for science imaging or spectrography. From this analysis we identify key technologies, like cryogenic electronics, Ethernet based interfaces and fully-digital detectors, for implementing efficient control of many detectors. We also propose a concept joining all identified technologies that could be considered for future large ESO instruments as a complement of ESO's general detector controller, NGCII.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes detector control strategies for large astronomical instruments, identifying key technologies like cryogenic electronics and Ethernet interfaces, and proposes a concept for future ESO instruments.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in controlling large arrays of detectors while considering scalability, cost, and sustainability for next-generation astronomical instruments.
AI.method: Review and analyze existing detector control approaches in recent ground-based instruments with 10+ detectors, focusing on technologies and their effectiveness.
AI.result: Identified key technologies (cryogenic electronics, Ethernet interfaces, fully-digital detectors) and proposed a整合 concept for future ESO instruments complementing NGCII.
AI.conclusion: The proposed integration of identified technologies offers a sustainable and efficient solution for detector control in upcoming large-scale astronomical projects.

title: Non-Schwarzschild black holes sourced by scalar-vector fields
summary: In a scalar-vector-gravity theory with the vector sector described by nonlinear electrodynamics, the field equations are integrated using the well-known gravitational decoupling method. The resulting spacetime corresponds to a spherically symmetric and static non-Schwarzschild black hole. Employing the master equations for both even and odd parity modes, it is proven that the solution is stable under certain conditions satisfied by the scalar and vector field parameters. To further corroborate the theoretical feasibility of this toy model, the causal structure, geodesic motion for massive particles, and some thermodynamic features are analyzed in detail.

AI.tldr: The paper examines a non-Schwarzschild black hole model using a scalar-vector-gravity theory with nonlinear electrodynamics. It confirms stability under specific parameter conditions and investigates causal structure, geodesic motion, and thermodynamics.
AI.motivation: To explore alternatives to the Schwarzschild black hole solution by incorporating nonlinear electrodynamics and scalar-vector interactions, ensuring stability through parity mode analysis and validating the model via causal structure, geodesics, and thermodynamic analysis.
AI.method: The gravitational decoupling method integrates field equations in a scalar-vector-gravity theory with nonlinear electrodynamics. Stability is proven using master equations for even and odd parity modes. Causal structure, geodesic motion, and thermodynamics are analyzed through standard spacetime and thermodynamic calculations.
AI.result: The constructed spacetime represents a stable spherically symmetric, static non-Schwarzschild black hole under specified scalar/vector field parameters. Analysis confirms valid causal structure, particle trajectories, and thermodynamic properties consistent with theoretical expectations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed toy model provides a viable alternative to Schwarzschild black holes within modified gravity frameworks, demonstrating stability and physical consistency. Further exploration of its astrophysical implications is encouraged.

title: Multi-Component Dark Matter as a Solution to the Galactic Center GeV Excess
summary: The Galactic Center Excess (GCE) is a compelling signature of dark matter annihilation, but its spectral morphology is difficult to reconcile with the traditional paradigm of a single particle species. In this work, we perform a systematic investigation of multi-component dark matter sectors, exploring scenarios with two ($N=2$) and three ($N=3$) distinct particle species while considering both exclusive and mixed annihilation channels. Using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) to rigorously penalize model complexity, we find that the GCE data statistically favors an $N=2$ scenario where each dark matter component annihilates exclusively into a single final state. Our results reveal that the preferred solutions naturally follow a light-plus-heavy mass hierarchy, and that specific final states such as $t\bar{t}$, $ZZ$, and $hh$, which are individually unable to explain the excess are effectively ``resurrected'' by the improved morphological fit provided by the multi-component framework. Furthermore, we show that these scenarios may mitigate the tension with current constraints, reaching compatibility within existing uncertainties. Our results suggest that the GCE may be the first evidence of a diverse dark sector, favoring a multi-scale solution over the minimal WIMP paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper explores multi-component dark matter sectors to explain the Galactic Center Excess (GCE), finding that a two-component model with exclusive annihilation channels provides a better fit than single-component models. Preferred mass hierarchies and specific final states resurrected by this approach suggest the GCE could indicate a diverse dark sector.
AI.motivation: The traditional single-particle dark matter model struggles to explain the GCE's spectral morphology. Multi-component scenarios are investigated to better fit the data and potentially resolve tensions with existing constraints.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of N=2 and N=3 dark matter components using AIC to penalize complexity. Examines exclusive and mixed annihilation channels, evaluating their ability to explain the GCE's spectrum.
AI.result: N=2 models with exclusive annihilation into specific states (tt̄, ZZ, hh) produce statistically better fits. Light-plus-heavy mass hierarchies are favored, and previously incompatible final states become viable within multi-component frameworks. Some constraints are alleviated.
AI.conclusion: The GCE may signal a multi-component dark sector rather than a single WIMP. Multi-scale models offer a superior explanation and suggest future particle physics searches should consider non-minimal scenarios.

title: State-dependent broadband X-ray timing reconfiguration in the changing-look AGN NGC 1566
summary: NGC 1566 has exhibited dramatic state changes in its X-ray spectrum, but the evolution of its broadband timing properties remains poorly constrained. We combine long-term Swift monitoring with high-time-resolution XMM-Newton observations to model the broadband X-ray power spectral density (PSD) in the dim and bright states. In the hard band, the PSD bend frequency shifts by about 1 dex between the two states, implying a substantially longer characteristic variability timescale in the bright state. The relative timing behaviour of the soft and hard bands also changes with state. In the dim state, the soft-band bend frequency is higher than the hard-band value by about 0.49 dex, whereas in the bright state the two become broadly consistent. The broadband variability evolution of NGC 1566 therefore involves not only an overall shift in characteristic timescale, but also a state-dependent change in the soft-hard timing relation, from a more stratified to a more tightly coupled configuration. Combined with previous spectral results, this supports a genuine reconfiguration of the inner radiative structure during the changing-look transition.

AI.tldr: The analysis of NGC 1566's X-ray variability reveals state-dependent changes in both the characteristic timescales and the soft-hard band timing relations, indicating a reconfiguration of the inner radiative structure during its changing-look transition.
AI.motivation: To investigate the evolution of broadband timing properties in NGC 1566 during its state changes, which were previously known for spectral variations but poorly understood in terms of timing behavior.
AI.method: Combined long-term Swift monitoring data with high-time-resolution XMM-Newton observations to model broadband X-ray power spectral densities (PSDs) in dim and bright states, analyzing band-dependent variability characteristics.
AI.result: Observed a 1 dex shift in hard band PSD bend frequency between states, indicating longer timescales in the bright state. Soft and hard band bend frequencies differ by 0.49 dex in the dim state but align in the bright state, showing a transition from stratified to coupled configurations.
AI.conclusion: The timing and spectral changes together suggest a genuine reconfiguration of the inner accretion disk structure during NGC 1566's changing-look transition, rather than just scaling of existing processes.

title: KMTNet Synoptic Survey of Southern Sky II: Data Reduction and Real-Time Transient Detection Pipeline
comment: 21 pages, 13 figures. Accepted for publication in the Journal of the Korean Astronomical Society (JKAS). Source code available at https://github.com/jmk5040/KMTNet_ToO
summary: We present a comprehensive pipeline developed for the image processing of the KMTNet Synoptic Survey of the Southern Sky (KS4) Data Release 1. This pipeline encompasses several key processes, including data quality assurance, astrometry, photometric zero-point (ZP) calibration, bad pixel masking, image stacking, and difference image analysis (DIA). The astrometric solutions were validated by cross-matching with the Gaia EDR3 catalog, achieving sub-pixel astrometric accuracy (< 0.4 arcsec). To ensure spatial consistency, we divided each image into multiple subsections and confirmed that astrometric accuracy was maintained even at the edges. We performed a two-stage photometric calibration. Initial ZP solutions were computed for each individual image frame using the APASS DR9 and SkyMapper DR3 catalogs. Subsequently, we corrected residual spatial variations in the stacked images using Gaia XP photometry. This procedure yielded a 5-sigma depth of 22-23 AB mag across the BVRI bands, with root-mean-square errors of approximately 0.03 mag when referenced to Gaia stars in the magnitude range of 14-19 mag. The processed KS4 images span over 4,000 deg^2 of the southern sky, providing reference images suitable for DIA. This publicly available pipeline also supports real-time processing of newly acquired images, enabling prompt transient detection. We demonstrate its effectiveness through successful applications in gravitational-wave follow-up observations.

AI.tldr: The paper details a comprehensive image processing pipeline for the KMTNet Synoptic Survey of the Southern Sky (KS4) Data Release 1, designed to achieve high astrometric and photometric accuracy through multi-stage calibration and validated with Gaia data. It enables large-scale transient detection and supports real-time analysis for gravitational-wave follow-up observations.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust pipeline ensuring precise astrometry and photometry for KS4 DR1, enabling wide-field imaging surveys and timely transient detection critical for applications like gravitational-wave follow-ups.
AI.method: The pipeline includes data quality assurance, astrometry via Gaia EDR3 cross-matching (<0.4 arcsec accuracy), two-stage photometric calibration (APASS/SkyMapper for initial ZP, Gaia XP for residual corrections), subsection-based astrometric validation, image stacking, and DIA. Processed images cover 4,000 deg² southern sky.
AI.result: Achieved sub-pixel astrometric precision, 22-23 AB mag 5σ depth in BVRI bands, and 0.03 mag RMS error in photometry. The pipeline successfully processed large regions and validated through gravitational-wave follow-up applications.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides reliable, publicly accessible tools for high-quality southern sky imaging, enabling both foundational surveys and rapid transient detection. Its real-time capabilities support cutting-edge astronomical event monitoring and response.

title: Electromagnetic radiation-reaction near black holes: orbital widening and the role of the tail
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures. Under review since 7 October 2025
summary: We investigate the orbital evolution of a classical charged particle around a Schwarzschild black hole immersed in an external, uniform magnetic field, taking into full account both local radiation-reaction and the nonlocal tail self-force arising in curved spacetime. Starting from the DeWitt-Brehme equation and its Landau-Lifshitz reduction, we derive analytic expressions for the conservative and dissipative components of the electromagnetic self-force in both the weak-field (Newtonian) and strong-field regimes. By implementing backward-in-time integration of the third-order DeWitt-Brehme equation alongside the second-order Landau-Lifshitz equation, we demonstrate that the so-called orbital widening effect persists even when the tail term is included, and that for astrophysically realistic charge-to-mass ratios the tail contribution to the trajectory is negligible. We further show that this widening is directly controlled by the product of the magnetic field and radiation-reaction parameters and can be captured in the Newtonian limit. Finally, we identify a scaling symmetry showing that simulations with moderate parameter values can accurately represent the dynamics in realistic astrophysical conditions, confirming that orbital widening is a robust phenomenon that can persist even in astrophysical black hole environments.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the orbital evolution of a charged particle around a Schwarzschild black hole in an external magnetic field, considering both local and nonlocal self-forces. It finds that the orbital widening effect persists even with tail self-force contributions, which are negligible for realistic charge-to-mass ratios. The effect is controlled by the product of magnetic and radiation-reaction parameters and remains robust under astrophysical conditions through identified scaling symmetries.
AI.motivation: To understand how electromagnetic self-forces (both conservative and dissipative) affect particle orbits around black holes in external magnetic fields, accounting for curved spacetime effects like tail terms. Prior work may have neglected the nonlocal tail contribution or used simplifying approximations.
AI.method: Derives analytic expressions using DeWitt-Brehke and Landau-Lifshitz equations for self-forces in weak and strong fields. Implements backward-in-time integration for the DeWitt-Brehme equation alongside Landau-Lifshitz to compare effects. Parameter analysis with astrophysically realistic charge-to-mass ratios is conducted. Scaling symmetry is identified to relate theoretical simulations to astrophysical scales.
AI.result: Orbital widening effect (previously noted in Landau-Lifshitz) persists when including tail terms. Tail contribution is negligible for realistic parameters. Widening depends on product of magnetic field and radiation-reaction parameters. The effect's robustness is confirmed via scaling symmetry allowing simulations at moderate parameters to reflect real astrophysical conditions.
AI.conclusion: Nonlocal tail effects do not significantly alter the qualitative orbital widening phenomenon in realistic scenarios. The findings validate the use of simpler models (like Landau-Lifshitz) for astrophysical applications, and demonstrate that electromagnetic self-forces produce detectable orbital effects around black holes even under magnetic field influences.

title: Hadronic Lorentz violation in chiral perturbation theory
comment: Presented at the Tenth Meeting on CPT and Lorentz Symmetry, Bloomington, Indiana, May 15-19, 2025
summary: Lorentz violation in hadronic systems is related to Lorentz-violating operators of quarks and gluons. Due to the nonperturbative nature of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) at low energies, establishing these relationships is complex. Chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) is an effective theory that provides one method of connecting quark- and gluon-level operators to those at the hadronic level, which can be used to calculate hadronic observables.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the use of chiral perturbation theory (ChPT) to bridge the gap between Lorentz-violating operators in quarks/gluons and measurable hadronic observables, addressing the complexity caused by QCD's nonperturbative nature at low energies.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges posed by the nonperturbative QCD regime in studying Lorentz violation in hadronic systems, enabling connections between fundamental Lorentz-violating operators and observable effects.
AI.method: Utilizes chiral perturbation theory as an effective field theory to systematically derive hadronic-level Lorentz-violating operators from quark and gluon-level operators, allowing calculations of observables like meson masses or decay constants.
AI.result: Provides a framework that translates quantifiable hadronic observables into constraints on Lorentz-violating coefficients at the fundamental level, facilitating experimental tests.
AI.conclusion: ChPT offers a viable and necessary tool for studying Lorentz violation in hadronic systems where direct QCD calculations are infeasible, opening pathways for future precision measurements and theoretical exploration.

title: Revealing the Spectral Properties of Galactic Interstellar Medium by Survey Observations
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: Based on multi-frequency radio polarization survey datasets, we investigate the spectral characteristics of the Galactic interstellar medium (ISM) using the polarization frequency analysis (PFA) method, referred to as polarization variance. By comparing this novel PFA technique with the traditional power spectrum approach, and by cross-examining data from two distinct surveys, we aim to reinforce the robustness of our findings. Our analysis reveals that the ISM scaling slope in the Galactic disk is steeper than the classic Kolmogorov slope, whereas the ISM scaling slope in the Galactic halo aligns with the Kolmogorov slope. We suggest a distinct turbulence cascade process operating in the Galactic halo compared to the Galactic disk.

AI.tldr: The study uses a new polarization variance method (PFA) to analyze multi-frequency radio polarization data of the Galactic interstellar medium, finding that the ISM scaling slope steepens in the Galactic disk compared to the Kolmogorov slope, while the halo follows Kolmogorov turbulence dynamics
AI.motivation: To assess the turbulence cascade differences between the Galactic disk and halo, improving on traditional power spectrum methods by using a novel polarization variance technique and cross-verifying with multiple datasets
AI.method: Applied polarization frequency analysis (PFA) method to multi-frequency polarization surveys, comparing with power spectrum techniques and cross-analyzing data from two distinct radio surveys
AI.result: Galactic disk ISM shows a steeper scaling slope than Kolmogorov (-11/3), while the halo matches the Kolmogorov slope, indicating different turbulence processes
AI.conclusion: The distinct turbulence dynamics between disk (steeper slope) and halo (Kolmogorov slope) suggest different physical mechanisms at work in these Galactic regions

title: Long-algorithm based quantum search for gravitational wave
summary: Gravitational wave astronomy is rapidly advancing with the development of new observatories, leading to an increasing volume and complexity of data. This trend places growing pressure on classical data analysis methods and motivates the exploration of quantum approaches. In this work, we introduce a quantum matched filtering framework for gravitational-wave detection based on the Long algorithm, marking its first application to the gravitational-wave data analysis. Numerical simulations show that the proposed approach preserves the quadratic speedup of quantum search while exhibiting significantly improved robustness, thereby overcoming key limitations of the Grover-algorithm based methods.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a quantum matched filtering framework for gravitational-wave detection using the Long algorithm, offering quadratic speedup and improved robustness compared to Grover-based methods.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth of gravitational wave data from new observatories challenges classical analysis methods, necessitating quantum approaches for better efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Adaptation of the Long algorithm into a quantum matched filtering framework for the first time in gravitational-wave data analysis.
AI.result: Numerical simulations demonstrate preserved quadratic speedup of quantum search and enhanced robustness over Grover-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum framework addresses key limitations of previous quantum methods, providing a more reliable approach for gravitational-wave detection.

title: Non-linear instability of the Kerr Cauchy horizon near $i_+$
summary:
We consider solutions of the Einstein vacuum equations which arise from smooth initial data on a hypersurface slightly inside a dynamical black hole settling down to a subextremal Kerr black hole, and satisfying a precise non-linear Price's law-type estimate (which we expect to hold generically). We prove that the corresponding maximal globally hyperbolic development admits a non-trivial piece of future null boundary - the Cauchy horizon - emanating from timelike infinity $i_+$, which exhibits a kind of curvature blow-up, and across which the spacetime metric is Lipschitz-inextendible. Our results thus imply a Lipschitz version of Strong Cosmic Censorship for Kerr spacetimes near timelike infinity under this Price's law-type assumption.
  The analysis relies on the proof of the $C^0$ stability of the Kerr Cauchy horizon by Dafermos and Luk, on the non-integrable formalism of Giorgi-Klainerman-Szeftel and principal temporal gauge of Klainerman and Szeftel used in the proof of the exterior stability of slowly rotating Kerr black holes, on the linearized analysis for the Teukolsky equation inside subextremal Kerr black holes by the author, and on Sbierski's criterion for Lipschitz inextendibility. More precisely, we proceed by decomposing the black hole interior into different regions equipped with appropriate gauges, allowing for a proof of stability estimates and a thorough analysis of the non-linear analog of the Teukolsky equation, from which we infer our instability results.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that under certain conditions, the Cauchy horizon in near-extremal Kerr black holes exhibits curvature blow-up and metric Lipschitz-inextendibility, supporting a Lipschitz version of the Strong Cosmic Censorship conjecture.
AI.motivation: To investigate the validity of the Strong Cosmic Censorship conjecture by analyzing the structure of spacetime near the Cauchy horizon in dynamical black holes settling to subextremal Kerr geometries.
AI.method: Combining existing frameworks including the $C^0$ stability of the Kerr Cauchy horizon, non-integrable formalism, principal temporal gauge, linearized Teukolsky equation analysis, and Sbierski's criterion to analyze the black hole interior across different regions and gauges.
AI.result: Proves that the Cauchy horizon is Lipschitz-inextendible with curvature blow-up, confirming the Lipschitz version of Strong Cosmic Censorship under the assumed Price's law-type condition.
AI.conclusion: The results strengthen the case for cosmic censorship by showing that the spacetime metric cannot be extended past the Cauchy horizon in a physically meaningful way under these conditions.

title: Impact of New Physics on the JUNO-Long-Baseline Synergy in Neutrino Mass Ordering Determination
summary: The determination of the neutrino mass ordering is one of the flagship goals in particle physics. A well-known and powerful synergy emerges when combining high-precision measurements of the effective atmospheric mass-squared splitting from electron antineutrino disappearance in reactor experiments with that from muon (anti)neutrino disappearance in accelerator-based long-baseline experiments. To fully exploit this synergy, percent-level precision in the atmospheric mass splitting is required-a target that JUNO is expected to achieve within a few months of data taking. This motivated the formulation of a mass ordering sum rule for neutrino disappearance channels, which shows that by combining data from T2K and NOvA with JUNO after one year of operation, the neutrino mass ordering can be determined at the $3σ$ confidence level. Since JUNO has recently started taking data, it is timely to ask whether this sum rule remains robust in the presence of new physics. We identify the necessary conditions for new physics to affect the sum rule and demonstrate that, in some cases, such effects could lead to an incorrect inference of the mass ordering. As concrete examples, we consider Scalar Non-Standard Interactions (SNSI) and neutrinos coupled to an ultralight scalar field. We find that, for SNSI, current constraints render any modification of the sum rule negligible, whereas in the latter case, the inference of the ordering requires caution. Nevertheless, these effects can be disentangled, illustrating how the sum rule can also be used to search for new physics.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the robustness of a neutrino mass ordering sum rule when considering new physics effects, using JUNO, T2K, and NOvA data. It finds some scenarios where new physics might mislead mass ordering inference, particularly with ultralight scalar fields, while SNSI impacts are negligible.
AI.motivation: To assess whether the synergy between reactor (JUNO) and accelerator (T2K/NOvA) experiments for determining neutrino mass ordering remains valid under potential new physics effects.
AI.method: Analyze conditions where new physics affects the sum rule; test specific cases (SNSI and ultralight scalar couplings); compare their impacts on mass ordering determination.
AI.result: SNSI constraints limit its effect on the sum rule, but ultralight scalar interactions could cause incorrect mass ordering inference. Disentangling these effects is possible, allowing new physics detection.
AI.conclusion: The sum rule remains mostly robust but requires caution in certain new physics scenarios. This analysis also provides a method to probe beyond-standard-model physics through mass ordering studies.

title: Optical transients from non-explosive double white-dwarf mergers: the case of a central neutron star remnant
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures, Accepted for publication in JHEAP
summary: Discoveries of ultra-massive magnetic white dwarfs (WDs) and peculiar pulsars have been proposed to originate in double white dwarf (DWD) mergers. There are three possible post-merger central remnants of non-explosive mergers: 1) a stable sub-Chandrasekhar WD; 2) a rapidly rotating super-Chandrasekhar WD; 3) a neutron star (NS). In this work, we explore the thermal transient arising from non-explosive DWD mergers that leave an NS remnant from the prompt collapse of the merged core. The transient is powered by the cooling of the expanding dynamical ejecta, with energy injection from magnetic dipole radiation, which depends on the dipole factor $D = B_d^2/P_0^4$, with $B_d$ and $P_0$ being the surface magnetic field strength and initial rotation period of the newborn NS. We simulate lightcurves in the Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) bands and estimate the horizon and detection rates for these transients across a range of model parameters. We find LSST detection horizons upper limits ranging $30$--$1020$ Mpc and corresponding detection rates $10^2$--$10^6$ yr$^{-1}$ for $\log D = 24$--$40$. Accounting for the survey cadence, we find that only configurations with $\log D = 36$--$40$ are detectable within $240$--$990$ Mpc, with detection rates $10^4$--$10^6$ yr$^{-1}$. Combined searches across surveys can compensate for the low cadence and improve the detection rates of fast and less energetic sources. Multi-wavelength campaigns can aid in detecting the spindown radiation at higher energies observable after the optical transient. Observations of these transients will provide direct evidence of the non-explosive DWD mergers, characterise the remnants and progenitor parameters, and the fraction of explosive and non-explosive mergers.

AI.tldr: This paper explores thermal transients from non-explosive double white dwarf (DWD) mergers leading to neutron star (NS) remnants. The study simulates optical lightcurves using LSST bands, finding detectability ranges (30-1020 Mpc) and rates (10²-10⁶ per year) dependent on the magnetic dipole factor D. Detectable configurations with log D=36–40 have improved detectability within 240–990 Mpc.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of peculiar astrophysical objects like ultra-massive magnetic white dwarfs and pulsars, which are proposed to form via DWD mergers. The goal is to characterize non-explosive merger outcomes (e.g., NS formation) through observable thermal transients.
AI.method: Simulated optical lightcurves for NS-forming DWD mergers using LSST bands, considering energy injection from NS magnetic dipole radiation (dependent on D = B_d²/P₀⁴). Evaluated detection horizons and rates across parameters, accounting for survey cadence.
AI.result: Detection horizons vary from 30–1,020 Mpc with rates 10²–10⁶ yr⁻¹ for log D=24–40. Only log D=36–40 systems are detectable within 240–990 Mpc at higher rates (10⁴–10⁶ yr⁻¹). Multi-wavelength observations are suggested to enhance detection of fast/low-energy sources.
AI.conclusion: LSST and multi-survey collaborations can confirm non-explosive DWD mergers, characterize NS remnants and progenitors, and measure merger outcomes' explosion fractions via these transients' observability.

title: Test of a 34 GHz EOM laser frequency comb at ESPRESSO
summary: Laser frequency combs (LFCs) are a promising technology for wavelength calibration of astronomical high-resolution spectrographs requiring utmost accuracy and stability, since they directly translate the fundamental SI time standard from the radio frequency regime to optical frequencies. However, they have so far seen limited use in practice, due to their complexity, incomplete wavelength coverage, but also the challenges in the data analysis they imply. Here, we present a detailed test of a 34 GHz electro-optic modulation comb with the ESPRESSO spectrograph.
